member.name: T:LegalChain.Api.ApiExceptionMiddleware
member.summary: Uniform 4xx error bodies ({"error": ...}) for rule violations and bad input — the C# analogue of the Java node's @RestControllerAdvice handler. (broken contract/ledger rules) maps to 400, (failed authenticity checks) to 403.

member.name: T:LegalChain.Api.ChainController
member.summary: The general ledger view: read the chain, audit it, and seal new blocks.

member.name: M:LegalChain.Api.ChainController.Validate
member.summary: Full independent audit: hashes, links, Merkle roots, signatures, consensus proofs.

member.name: M:LegalChain.Api.ChainController.Mine(LegalChain.Api.ChainController.MineRequest)
member.summary: Seals pending transactions into a block using the active consensus strategy.

member.name: T:LegalChain.Api.ConsensusController
member.summary: Inspect and switch the active consensus strategy (Strategy pattern, live).

member.name: T:LegalChain.Api.ContractController
member.summary: Sector smart contracts: medical consent registry and agri supply-chain traceability.

member.name: T:LegalChain.Api.I18nController
member.summary: Server-side dictionaries backing the mandatory PL/EN language switcher: the frontend keeps its own UI dictionaries, and this endpoint serves the shared, backend-owned strings (consensus/PQC descriptions) so both nodes present identical wording.

member.name: T:LegalChain.Api.NftController
member.summary: NFT minting and gallery: on-chain certification of digital ownership + SSI.

member.name: T:LegalChain.Api.NodeController
member.summary: Node identity and P2P operations (connect to a peer, trigger a full ledger sync).

member.name: M:LegalChain.Api.NodeController.Connect(LegalChain.Api.NodeController.ConnectRequest)
member.summary: Opens a PQC-secured link to a remote peer, e.g. ws://localhost:8101/ws/p2p.

member.name: M:LegalChain.Api.NodeController.Sync
member.summary: Requests the ledger from all established peers; longest valid chain wins locally.

member.name: T:LegalChain.Api.TransactionController
member.summary: Token transfers. In the MVP the browser has no key store, so the node wallet signs on the user's behalf (custodial model); the ML-DSA signature is created here and verified again by — the ledger never trusts the API layer.

member.name: T:LegalChain.Api.WalletController
member.summary: This node's post-quantum wallet (self-sovereign identity) and the derived balances.

member.name: M:LegalChain.Api.WalletController.Balances
member.summary: All balances, derived by replaying the ledger — never stored, always auditable.

member.name: T:LegalChain.Config.LegalChainOptions
member.summary: Node configuration bound from the LegalChain section of appsettings.json, overridable per node via CLI switches (--port, --node-name) — the same shape used by the Java (application.yml) and C++ (application.json) nodes.

member.name: P:LegalChain.Config.LegalChainOptions.NodeName
member.summary: Human-readable node name; the cryptographic node identity is the ML-DSA public-key fingerprint generated at startup (see NodeWallet).

member.name: T:LegalChain.Config.LegalChainOptions.TokenomicsOptions
member.summary: Educational tokenomics: fixed block reward, halved every blocks, with a hard supply cap.

member.name: T:LegalChain.Consensus.BftStrategy
member.summary: Byzantine Fault Tolerance (pBFT-style, simulated with an in-process validator panel).

remarks.para: The classical result: with n = 3f + 1 validators, agreement is guaranteed even if f of them are arbitrarily malicious ("Byzantine"), because any two quorums of 2f + 1 votes intersect in at least one honest validator. Here n = 4, f = 1: a block is sealed only when at least 3 of the 4 panel members approve the candidate's Merkle root. BFT gives immediate finality (no probabilistic confirmations, no forks), which is precisely the property permissioned, regulated ledgers need — a court or auditor can treat an appended entry as final.

Votes are simulated deterministically (each validator independently recomputes and signs off on the candidate hash), so peers can re-validate the recorded quorum. In a production network each vote would be an ML-DSA signature from a distinct node.

member.name: T:LegalChain.Consensus.ConsensusEngine
member.summary: Holds the registry of implementations and the currently active one. The ASP.NET Core container injects every registered strategy, so a new consensus algorithm becomes available API-wide by simply registering one more implementation of the interface.

member.name: T:LegalChain.Consensus.IConsensusStrategy
member.summary: Strategy pattern for consensus: the ledger core is agnostic about how a block earns the right to be appended. Each implementation seals a candidate (producing its proof) and can independently re-validate any sealed block — validation must be a pure function of the block, because remote nodes re-check proofs during P2P sync without trusting the sender.
member.remarks: This is the "Consensus and Scalability" seam of the architecture: adding DPoS, Proof-of-Reputation, PoH or PoET later means adding one class, not touching the core. (Named ConsensusStrategy in the Java and C++ nodes; the leading I is C# idiom.)

member.name: P:LegalChain.Consensus.IConsensusStrategy.Name
member.summary: Stable identifier used in the API and stored in each block (consensusType).

member.name: P:LegalChain.Consensus.IConsensusStrategy.Description
member.summary: One-line explanation surfaced by the educational frontend.

member.name: M:LegalChain.Consensus.IConsensusStrategy.Seal(LegalChain.Core.BlockCandidate)
member.summary: Produces the proof for the candidate and returns the sealed, hashed block.

member.name: M:LegalChain.Consensus.IConsensusStrategy.Validate(LegalChain.Core.Block)
member.summary: Re-verifies the proof of a sealed block (used when auditing chains from peers).

member.name: T:LegalChain.Consensus.ProofOfStakeStrategy
member.summary: Proof of Stake: the right to seal a block is assigned by stake-weighted lottery instead of computational work.

remarks.para: The lottery must be deterministic and grinding-resistant so that every node (including a peer receiving the block during sync) selects the same winner: we seed it with the previous block hash — a value the sealer cannot freely re-roll — and walk the cumulative stake distribution. Security intuition: attacking the network requires owning a large share of the very asset the attack would devalue ("skin in the game"), and misbehaviour can be punished by slashing stake. Energy cost is negligible, which is why PoS is the default strategy of this compliance-oriented ledger (ESG reporting is part of regulatory reality).
MVP simplification: the validator set is a fixed, documented registry; production systems derive it from on-chain staking transactions.

member.name: F:LegalChain.Consensus.ProofOfStakeStrategy.Stakes
member.summary: Demo validator registry: address → staked tokens (insertion order matters for determinism).

member.name: M:LegalChain.Consensus.ProofOfStakeStrategy.SelectValidator(System.String)
member.summary: Deterministic stake-weighted selection seeded by the previous block hash.

member.name: T:LegalChain.Consensus.ProofOfWorkStrategy
member.summary: Proof of Work: the sealer increments a nonce until the block hash falls below a difficulty target (here: a required prefix of zero hex digits).
member.remarks: Security intuition: rewriting history requires redoing the accumulated work of every later block faster than the honest network extends it — economically infeasible past a few confirmations. The proof is trivially verifiable (one hash) but expensive to produce, an asymmetry that needs no identities at all. Trade-off: enormous energy cost, which is why this MVP defaults to PoS and keeps difficulty low (4 hex zeros ≈ 65k hashes) for classroom responsiveness.

member.name: T:LegalChain.Contracts.AgriSupplyChainContract
member.summary: Agriculture-sector contract: farm-to-fork supply chain transparency.
member.remarks: Each batch of produce accumulates an ordered, immutable trail of stage events (harvest → processing → transport → warehouse → retail). The contract enforces that stages only move forward — a shipment cannot "return" to the farm on paper, which is precisely the class of document fraud this eliminates. Because every event is a signed ledger transaction, any consumer, importer or food-safety authority (e.g. under EU regulation 178/2002 traceability requirements) can audit the full provenance of a batch without trusting any single participant's database.

member.name: F:LegalChain.Contracts.AgriSupplyChainContract.Stages
member.summary: Ordered lifecycle stages; index = position in the lifecycle.

member.name: M:LegalChain.Contracts.AgriSupplyChainContract.TrailOf(System.String)
member.summary: Full provenance trail of a batch — the consumer/auditor view.

member.name: T:LegalChain.Contracts.ContractEngine
member.summary: Executes an and anchors the result on-chain: the contract's output record becomes the payload of a ledger transaction signed with this node's ML-DSA wallet. The chain therefore holds a signed, timestamped, tamper-evident proof of every contract execution — the "code is auditable law" property of Blockchain 3.0.

member.name: T:LegalChain.Contracts.ContractResult
member.summary: Outcome of a smart-contract execution: the on-chain record (payload) plus a human-readable message for the UI.
member.param: which contract produced this result
whether the state transition was applied
human-readable outcome (shown in the frontend)
the exact key/value data recorded in the ledger transaction
id of the ledger transaction recording this execution (set by the engine)

member.name: M:LegalChain.Contracts.ContractResult.#ctor(System.String,System.Boolean,System.String,System.Collections.Generic.IReadOnlyDictionary{System.String,System.String},System.String)
member.summary: Outcome of a smart-contract execution: the on-chain record (payload) plus a human-readable message for the UI.
member.param: which contract produced this result
whether the state transition was applied
human-readable outcome (shown in the frontend)
the exact key/value data recorded in the ledger transaction
id of the ledger transaction recording this execution (set by the engine)

member.name: P:LegalChain.Contracts.ContractResult.ContractId
member.summary: which contract produced this result

member.name: P:LegalChain.Contracts.ContractResult.Accepted
member.summary: whether the state transition was applied

member.name: P:LegalChain.Contracts.ContractResult.Message
member.summary: human-readable outcome (shown in the frontend)

member.name: P:LegalChain.Contracts.ContractResult.Record
member.summary: the exact key/value data recorded in the ledger transaction

member.name: P:LegalChain.Contracts.ContractResult.TxId
member.summary: id of the ledger transaction recording this execution (set by the engine)

member.name: T:LegalChain.Contracts.ISmartContract
member.summary: Blockchain 3.0 smart contract abstraction: deterministic business logic whose every execution is recorded as a signed ledger transaction.
member.remarks: Design rule for compliance (GDPR art. 17 "right to erasure" vs. immutability): contracts must keep personal data off-chain and record on-chain only hashes, pseudonymous identifiers and consent/state transitions. The chain then proves that and when something happened without ever containing erasable personal data — the standard pattern that reconciles blockchains with GDPR. (Named SmartContract in the Java and C++ nodes; the leading I is C# idiom.)

member.name: P:LegalChain.Contracts.ISmartContract.ContractId
member.summary: Stable contract identifier recorded in the transaction payload.

member.name: P:LegalChain.Contracts.ISmartContract.Description
member.summary: Sector / purpose description surfaced by the educational frontend.

member.name: M:LegalChain.Contracts.ISmartContract.Execute(System.Collections.Generic.IReadOnlyDictionary{System.String,System.String})
member.summary: Validates the input against the contract's rules and returns the state transition to be recorded on-chain. Implementations must be deterministic and side-effect free apart from their own state registry (same input → same result on every node).
member.exception: if the input violates contract rules

member.name: T:LegalChain.Contracts.MedicalConsentContract
member.summary: Medical-sector contract: consent-based access to patient history.
member.remarks: How it stays GDPR-compliant on an immutable ledger: no medical data and no real identity ever touches the chain. The contract records only pseudonymous identifiers (patientId / granteeId are opaque strings — in production, DID key fingerprints), a consent scope and a grant/revoke flag. The chain thus becomes a tamper-proof consent audit trail: a hospital can prove it held valid consent at the time of access (GDPR art. 7 accountability), and revocation is itself an immutable event — consent history can never be silently rewritten.

member.name: F:LegalChain.Contracts.MedicalConsentContract.Scopes
member.summary: Allowed consent scopes — a closed vocabulary keeps records machine-auditable.

member.name: F:LegalChain.Contracts.MedicalConsentContract._consents
member.summary: Current consent state per patient (rebuilt from chain in a production system).

member.name: M:LegalChain.Contracts.MedicalConsentContract.HistoryOf(System.String)
member.summary: Full consent history for a patient — the audit trail view.

member.name: M:LegalChain.Contracts.MedicalConsentContract.HasConsent(System.String,System.String,System.String)
member.summary: Access check used by hypothetical medical systems: latest record wins.

member.name: T:LegalChain.Core.Block
member.summary: Immutable block of the general ledger (C# record).
member.remarks: The block hash covers the index, timestamp, previous hash, Merkle root, validator, consensus proof and nonce. Because previousHash of block N+1 is the hash of block N, retroactively editing any historical transaction invalidates every later block — this hash chaining is the core tamper-evidence property that makes a blockchain acceptable as a compliant, auditable registry.
member.param: position in the chain (genesis = 0)
epoch millis when the block was sealed
hash of the preceding block (64 zeros for genesis)
Merkle root committing to all transactions in this block
the transactions recorded in this block
address of the node that sealed the block (receives the reward)
name of the consensus strategy that sealed this block
strategy-specific proof (PoW target, PoS selection, BFT votes)
PoW counter (0 for non-PoW strategies)
SHA3-256 hash of the block header

member.name: M:LegalChain.Core.Block.#ctor(System.Int64,System.Int64,System.String,System.String,System.Collections.Generic.IReadOnlyList{LegalChain.Core.Transaction},System.String,System.String,System.String,System.Int64,System.String)
member.summary: Immutable block of the general ledger (C# record).
member.remarks: The block hash covers the index, timestamp, previous hash, Merkle root, validator, consensus proof and nonce. Because previousHash of block N+1 is the hash of block N, retroactively editing any historical transaction invalidates every later block — this hash chaining is the core tamper-evidence property that makes a blockchain acceptable as a compliant, auditable registry.
member.param: position in the chain (genesis = 0)
epoch millis when the block was sealed
hash of the preceding block (64 zeros for genesis)
Merkle root committing to all transactions in this block
the transactions recorded in this block
address of the node that sealed the block (receives the reward)
name of the consensus strategy that sealed this block
strategy-specific proof (PoW target, PoS selection, BFT votes)
PoW counter (0 for non-PoW strategies)
SHA3-256 hash of the block header

member.name: P:LegalChain.Core.Block.Index
member.summary: position in the chain (genesis = 0)

member.name: P:LegalChain.Core.Block.Timestamp
member.summary: epoch millis when the block was sealed

member.name: P:LegalChain.Core.Block.PreviousHash
member.summary: hash of the preceding block (64 zeros for genesis)

member.name: P:LegalChain.Core.Block.MerkleRoot
member.summary: Merkle root committing to all transactions in this block

member.name: P:LegalChain.Core.Block.Transactions
member.summary: the transactions recorded in this block

member.name: P:LegalChain.Core.Block.ValidatorId
member.summary: address of the node that sealed the block (receives the reward)

member.name: P:LegalChain.Core.Block.ConsensusType
member.summary: name of the consensus strategy that sealed this block

member.name: P:LegalChain.Core.Block.Proof
member.summary: strategy-specific proof (PoW target, PoS selection, BFT votes)

member.name: P:LegalChain.Core.Block.Nonce
member.summary: PoW counter (0 for non-PoW strategies)

member.name: P:LegalChain.Core.Block.Hash
member.summary: SHA3-256 hash of the block header

member.name: M:LegalChain.Core.Block.ComputeHash(System.Int64,System.Int64,System.String,System.String,System.String,System.String,System.String,System.Int64)
member.summary: Recomputes the header hash; used both when sealing and when auditing the chain.

member.name: M:LegalChain.Core.Block.HashIsConsistent
member.summary: True if the stored hash matches a recomputation of the header — tamper check.

member.name: T:LegalChain.Core.BlockCandidate
member.summary: An unsealed block handed to a , which produces the proof/nonce and the final . Separating the candidate from the sealed block is what lets consensus algorithms be swapped (Strategy pattern) without touching the ledger core.

member.name: M:LegalChain.Core.BlockCandidate.#ctor(System.Int64,System.Int64,System.String,System.Collections.Generic.IReadOnlyList{LegalChain.Core.Transaction},System.String)
member.summary: An unsealed block handed to a , which produces the proof/nonce and the final . Separating the candidate from the sealed block is what lets consensus algorithms be swapped (Strategy pattern) without touching the ledger core.

member.name: M:LegalChain.Core.BlockCandidate.Seal(System.String,System.String,System.Int64)
member.summary: Convenience for strategies: seal this candidate with the given proof and nonce.

member.name: T:LegalChain.Core.Blockchain
member.summary: The general ledger: an append-only, hash-chained list of blocks plus the pool of pending (signed, not yet sealed) transactions.

remarks.para: This is a real ledger, not a mock: balances are never stored — they are derived by replaying every transaction from genesis (), signatures are verified with ML-DSA before a transaction is accepted, and re-audits hashes, links, Merkle roots, signatures and consensus proofs of the entire history. That auditability-from-first-principles is the property that makes a blockchain acceptable as a compliant book of record.

Thread-safety: mutating operations take the chain lock; reads return defensive copies. Request handling rides on the async/await thread pool (the .NET analogue of Java's Loom virtual threads), so briefly blocked writers are cheap.

member.name: F:LegalChain.Core.Blockchain.Genesis
member.summary: Deterministic genesis: identical on every node, so two fresh remote nodes share a common root.

member.name: M:LegalChain.Core.Blockchain.Submit(LegalChain.Core.Transaction)
member.summary: Accepts a transaction into the pending pool after verifying its ML-DSA signature, that the claimed sender address really is the fingerprint of the signing key (otherwise anyone could sign as somebody else with their own key), and that the sender can actually cover the amount: confirmed balance minus what they already have pending. Rewards are exempt — they are the only sanctioned money creation, bounded by . Counting pending outgoing amounts closes the mempool double-spend: two transfers that are individually covered but jointly overdraw are caught at the second Submit, so no sealed block can ever drive a balance negative — the first invariant an auditor checks in any book of record.

member.name: M:LegalChain.Core.Blockchain.PendingOutgoingOf(System.String)
member.summary: The sender's not-yet-sealed outgoing total; call only while holding the chain lock.

member.name: M:LegalChain.Core.Blockchain.MinePendingTransactions(System.String)
member.summary: Seals the pending transactions into a new block via the active consensus strategy and credits the validator with the tokenomics reward.

member.name: M:LegalChain.Core.Blockchain.IsValidChain(System.Collections.Generic.IReadOnlyList{LegalChain.Core.Block})
member.summary: Full audit of a chain: genesis identity, hash consistency, previous-hash links, transaction signatures, and each block's own consensus proof (looked up by the strategy recorded in the block, so mixed-consensus histories stay verifiable).

member.name: M:LegalChain.Core.Blockchain.GenesisEquals(LegalChain.Core.Block)
member.summary: Genesis comparison by value: a deserialized peer genesis is a distinct instance.

member.name: M:LegalChain.Core.Blockchain.ReplaceChain(System.Collections.Generic.IReadOnlyList{LegalChain.Core.Block})
member.summary: Longest-valid-chain rule used by P2P sync: adopt a peer's chain only if it is strictly longer and passes the full local audit — a malicious peer cannot push a forged history, because we never trust, we always re-verify.

member.name: M:LegalChain.Core.Blockchain.Balances
member.summary: Balances derived by replaying the full ledger — the "general ledger" property.

member.name: T:LegalChain.Core.EventBus
member.summary: Minimal in-process publish/subscribe bus — the C# analogue of Spring's ApplicationEventPublisher used by the Java node. Subscribers (the /ws/events broadcaster and the P2P gossip) are registered at startup; publication is synchronous and exceptions in one subscriber never break the ledger operation that raised the event.

member.name: T:LegalChain.Core.LedgerEvent
member.summary: Domain event published by the ledger through the in-process . The WebSocket broadcaster relays these to the educational frontend at /ws/events so the UI can animate blocks and transactions the moment they happen.
member.remarks: Member names are UPPER_SNAKE_CASE because they travel verbatim on the wire.

member.name: M:LegalChain.Core.LedgerEvent.#ctor(LegalChain.Core.LedgerEvent.EventType,System.Object)
member.summary: Domain event published by the ledger through the in-process . The WebSocket broadcaster relays these to the educational frontend at /ws/events so the UI can animate blocks and transactions the moment they happen.
member.remarks: Member names are UPPER_SNAKE_CASE because they travel verbatim on the wire.

member.name: T:LegalChain.Core.NodeWallet
member.summary: The node's own post-quantum wallet: an ML-DSA-65 key pair generated at startup.
member.remarks: Self-sovereign identity in practice: the node's address is the SHA3-256 fingerprint of its public key. No registration authority issues it, no third party can revoke it, and it reveals nothing about the operator — yet every signature made with it is independently verifiable by anyone. The private key never leaves this process (in the MVP it is intentionally not persisted: restart = new identity).

member.name: T:LegalChain.Core.TokenomicsService
member.summary: Educational tokenomics: a fixed block reward that halves every N blocks under a hard supply cap (the Bitcoin issuance model, scaled down so halvings are observable in a classroom session).
member.remarks: Why this matters for compliance: the total supply and the issuance schedule are deterministic functions of the chain itself — no discretionary "printing". An auditor can recompute the entire monetary base from the ledger, which is exactly the transparency property regulators (e.g. under MiCA) expect from a token issuer.

member.name: M:LegalChain.Core.TokenomicsService.RewardForBlock(System.Int64,System.Decimal)
member.summary: Reward for the block at , after halvings and the supply cap.

member.name: M:LegalChain.Core.TokenomicsService.RewardTransaction(System.String,System.Int64,System.Decimal)
member.summary: Coinbase-style reward transaction crediting the sealing validator.

member.name: M:LegalChain.Core.TokenomicsService.IssuedSupply(System.Collections.Generic.IReadOnlyList{LegalChain.Core.Block})
member.summary: Total supply issued so far = sum of all REWARD transactions in the chain.

member.name: T:LegalChain.Core.Transaction
member.summary: Immutable ledger transaction (C# record — immutability is a compliance feature: once created, an entry cannot be mutated in memory, only superseded on-chain).
member.remarks: Every non-reward transaction is signed with the sender's ML-DSA key over , and carries the sender's public key so that any node or external auditor can verify authorship offline. The sender "address" is a SHA3-256 fingerprint of that key — pseudonymous, self-sovereign identity.
member.param: unique transaction id (UUID)
epoch millis at creation
sender address (key fingerprint) or for rewards
recipient address
token amount (zero for pure data transactions)
ledger entry category
type-specific data (NFT metadata, contract arguments, memo)
Base64 ML-DSA public key (null for REWARD)
Base64 ML-DSA signature over

member.name: M:LegalChain.Core.Transaction.#ctor(System.String,System.Int64,System.String,System.String,System.Decimal,LegalChain.Core.TransactionType,System.Collections.Generic.IReadOnlyDictionary{System.String,System.String},System.String,System.String)
member.summary: Immutable ledger transaction (C# record — immutability is a compliance feature: once created, an entry cannot be mutated in memory, only superseded on-chain).
member.remarks: Every non-reward transaction is signed with the sender's ML-DSA key over , and carries the sender's public key so that any node or external auditor can verify authorship offline. The sender "address" is a SHA3-256 fingerprint of that key — pseudonymous, self-sovereign identity.
member.param: unique transaction id (UUID)
epoch millis at creation
sender address (key fingerprint) or for rewards
recipient address
token amount (zero for pure data transactions)
ledger entry category
type-specific data (NFT metadata, contract arguments, memo)
Base64 ML-DSA public key (null for REWARD)
Base64 ML-DSA signature over

member.name: P:LegalChain.Core.Transaction.Id
member.summary: unique transaction id (UUID)

member.name: P:LegalChain.Core.Transaction.Timestamp
member.summary: epoch millis at creation

member.name: P:LegalChain.Core.Transaction.Sender
member.summary: sender address (key fingerprint) or for rewards

member.name: P:LegalChain.Core.Transaction.Recipient
member.summary: recipient address

member.name: P:LegalChain.Core.Transaction.Amount
member.summary: token amount (zero for pure data transactions)

member.name: P:LegalChain.Core.Transaction.Type
member.summary: ledger entry category

member.name: P:LegalChain.Core.Transaction.Payload
member.summary: type-specific data (NFT metadata, contract arguments, memo)

member.name: P:LegalChain.Core.Transaction.SenderPublicKey
member.summary: Base64 ML-DSA public key (null for REWARD)

member.name: P:LegalChain.Core.Transaction.Signature
member.summary: Base64 ML-DSA signature over

member.name: M:LegalChain.Core.Transaction.ContentToSign
member.summary: Canonical string covered by the ML-DSA signature. The payload map is serialized in sorted-key order so signer and verifier always derive the same bytes.

member.name: M:LegalChain.Core.Transaction.FormatAmount(System.Decimal)
member.summary: Canonical plain decimal rendering without trailing zeros (BigDecimal-style).

member.name: T:LegalChain.Core.TransactionType
member.summary: Ledger entry categories; contract types make sector use-cases auditable on-chain.
member.remarks: Member names are deliberately UPPER_SNAKE_CASE: they are serialized verbatim to JSON (API contract v1 shared with the Java and C++ nodes and the React frontend).

member.name: T:LegalChain.Crypto.HashUtil
member.summary: SHA3-256 hashing utilities for block hashes and Merkle roots.
member.remarks: Why SHA3-256: Grover's algorithm gives a quantum attacker at most a quadratic speed-up against hash pre-images, so a 256-bit sponge construction (Keccak) retains ~128-bit post-quantum security. Unlike the signature layer, the hash layer therefore needs no algorithm swap to stay quantum-resistant — which is why Bitcoin's SHA-256 chaining is not its quantum weakness (its ECDSA signatures are).

member.name: M:LegalChain.Crypto.HashUtil.Sha3(System.String)
member.summary: SHA3-256 of the UTF-8 bytes of , as lowercase hex.

member.name: M:LegalChain.Crypto.HashUtil.MerkleRoot(System.Collections.Generic.IReadOnlyList{System.String})
member.summary: Computes a Merkle root over the given leaf hashes. The root commits to every transaction in a block: changing any transaction changes the root, which changes the block hash, which breaks every subsequent previousHash link — this is what makes the ledger tamper-evident and auditable (a compliance requirement for a general ledger).

member.name: T:LegalChain.Crypto.PqcKeyExchangeService
member.summary: Post-quantum key establishment using ML-KEM-768 (NIST FIPS 203, standardized from CRYSTALS-Kyber), provided by the BouncyCastle.Cryptography library.

remarks.para: QKD-inspired design. True Quantum Key Distribution needs dedicated optical hardware, but its two security ideas can be reproduced in software: (1) the shared symmetric key is freshly established per session and never stored or reused, and (2) its secrecy does not depend on any key that a "harvest now, decrypt later" adversary could break retroactively — ML-KEM's security rests on the Module-LWE lattice problem, against which Shor's algorithm gives no advantage. Each P2P session therefore gets a one-time AES-256 key that a quantum-equipped eavesdropper recording today's traffic still cannot recover.

MITM resistance comes from binding this KEM exchange to ML-DSA identities: the handshake messages that carry the KEM public key and the ciphertext are signed with the sender's ML-DSA key (see P2p.P2pService), so an attacker cannot substitute their own KEM key without forging a post-quantum signature.

member.name: M:LegalChain.Crypto.PqcKeyExchangeService.Encapsulate(System.String)
member.summary: Sender side: encapsulates a fresh shared secret against the receiver's public key. Returns the ciphertext to transmit plus the derived AES-256 session key.

member.name: M:LegalChain.Crypto.PqcKeyExchangeService.Decapsulate(System.String,Org.BouncyCastle.Crypto.Parameters.MLKemPrivateKeyParameters)
member.summary: Receiver side: recovers the same AES-256 session key from the transmitted ciphertext.

member.name: T:LegalChain.Crypto.PqcKeyExchangeService.Encapsulation
member.summary: Result of KEM encapsulation: what travels on the wire + the local session key.

member.name: M:LegalChain.Crypto.PqcKeyExchangeService.Encapsulation.#ctor(System.String,System.Byte[])
member.summary: Result of KEM encapsulation: what travels on the wire + the local session key.

member.name: T:LegalChain.Crypto.PqcSignatureService
member.summary: Post-quantum digital signatures using ML-DSA-65 (NIST FIPS 204, standardized from CRYSTALS-Dilithium), provided by the BouncyCastle.Cryptography library.

remarks.para: Why this is compliant and secure: classical blockchain signatures (Bitcoin/Ethereum ECDSA over secp256k1) rely on the discrete-logarithm problem, which Shor's algorithm solves in polynomial time on a large fault-tolerant quantum computer. ML-DSA instead rests on the hardness of lattice problems (Module-LWE / Module-SIS), for which no quantum speed-up is known. NIST finalized it as FIPS 204 in August 2024; using a NIST-standardized parameter set (ML-DSA-65, NIST security category 3, ~AES-192 equivalent) is what makes the ledger auditable against a concrete regulatory benchmark rather than ad-hoc cryptography.

Every ledger transaction carries the sender's ML-DSA public key and signature, so any node — or any auditor — can independently verify authorship without contacting the author. Identity stays pseudonymous: the "address" is only a SHA3-256 fingerprint of the public key (self-sovereign identity), never a name.

member.name: P:LegalChain.Crypto.PqcSignatureService.AlgorithmName
member.summary: Human-readable algorithm label exposed over the API and in the education module.

member.name: M:LegalChain.Crypto.PqcSignatureService.Sign(System.String,Org.BouncyCastle.Crypto.Parameters.MLDsaPrivateKeyParameters)
member.summary: Signs UTF-8 content and returns the signature as Base64.

member.name: M:LegalChain.Crypto.PqcSignatureService.Verify(System.String,System.String,System.String)
member.summary: Verifies a Base64 signature against UTF-8 content and a Base64-encoded public key.

member.name: M:LegalChain.Crypto.PqcSignatureService.DecodePublicKey(System.String)
member.summary: Decodes a Base64 ML-DSA public key; throws if malformed.

member.name: M:LegalChain.Crypto.PqcSignatureService.Fingerprint(Org.BouncyCastle.Crypto.Parameters.MLDsaPublicKeyParameters)
member.summary: Pseudonymous address: SHA3-256 fingerprint of the encoded public key. This is the ZKP-flavoured privacy property of the ledger — parties prove control of a key (by producing valid ML-DSA signatures) without ever revealing a real-world identity.

member.name: T:LegalChain.Crypto.SecureChannel
member.summary: Authenticated symmetric channel: AES-256-GCM under the ML-KEM-derived session key.
member.remarks: GCM provides confidentiality and integrity in one primitive: any bit flipped in transit fails the authentication tag and the message is rejected, so a man-in-the-middle who cannot break the KEM cannot silently alter ledger data either. A fresh random 96-bit nonce is generated per message and prepended to the ciphertext (a nonce must never repeat under the same GCM key; since our session keys are one-time ML-KEM secrets, random nonces are safe at these message volumes).

member.name: M:LegalChain.Crypto.SecureChannel.Encrypt(System.String,System.Byte[])
member.summary: Encrypts UTF-8 plaintext; returns Base64(nonce || ciphertext || tag).

member.name: M:LegalChain.Crypto.SecureChannel.Decrypt(System.String,System.Byte[])
member.summary: Decrypts Base64(nonce || ciphertext || tag); throws if the authentication tag is invalid.

member.name: T:LegalChain.LegalChainApp
member.summary: Builds the fully wired LegalChain node as a . Extracted from Program so integration tests can boot two complete nodes (real Kestrel, real WebSockets) inside one process — the C# equivalent of the Java node's two Spring contexts in TwoNodeSyncIntegrationTest.
member.remarks: All services are singletons registered in dependency order (the same object graph as the Java node's Spring context and the C++ node's hand-wired AppContext): EventBus → crypto services → NodeWallet → consensus → tokenomics → Blockchain → contracts → NFT → P2P. ASP.NET Core's built-in container plays the role Spring plays in app01 — an instructive contrast with app02's explicit constructor wiring.

member.name: T:LegalChain.Nft.Nft
member.summary: A minted non-fungible token: on-chain certificate of digital ownership.
member.param: unique token id (UUID)
work title
work description
pointer to off-chain metadata/asset (e.g. ipfs://...) — the ledger stores only this URI and its provenance, never the asset
ML-DSA key fingerprint of the creator (self-sovereign identity)
epoch millis of minting
ledger transaction that certifies the mint

member.name: M:LegalChain.Nft.Nft.#ctor(System.String,System.String,System.String,System.String,System.String,System.Int64,System.String)
member.summary: A minted non-fungible token: on-chain certificate of digital ownership.
member.param: unique token id (UUID)
work title
work description
pointer to off-chain metadata/asset (e.g. ipfs://...) — the ledger stores only this URI and its provenance, never the asset
ML-DSA key fingerprint of the creator (self-sovereign identity)
epoch millis of minting
ledger transaction that certifies the mint

member.name: P:LegalChain.Nft.Nft.TokenId
member.summary: unique token id (UUID)

member.name: P:LegalChain.Nft.Nft.Title
member.summary: work title

member.name: P:LegalChain.Nft.Nft.Description
member.summary: work description

member.name: P:LegalChain.Nft.Nft.MetadataUri
member.summary: pointer to off-chain metadata/asset (e.g. ipfs://...) — the ledger stores only this URI and its provenance, never the asset

member.name: P:LegalChain.Nft.Nft.Creator
member.summary: ML-DSA key fingerprint of the creator (self-sovereign identity)

member.name: P:LegalChain.Nft.Nft.MintedAt
member.summary: epoch millis of minting

member.name: P:LegalChain.Nft.Nft.TxId
member.summary: ledger transaction that certifies the mint

member.name: T:LegalChain.Nft.NftService
member.summary: NFT minting: certifies digital ownership and creator identity (SSI) on the ledger.
member.remarks: How this certifies authorship without any registry or notary: the mint transaction is signed with the creator's ML-DSA key, and the creator's address is the fingerprint of that key. Anyone can verify that whoever controls that key minted this token at this time — self-sovereign identity in its purest form. The asset itself stays off-chain behind metadataUri (IPFS in production: a content-addressed URI, so the metadata is itself tamper-evident), keeping the chain lean and GDPR-safe.

member.name: T:LegalChain.P2p.EventBroadcaster
member.summary: Frontend live feed at /ws/events: relays every to all connected browsers as {"type": ..., "data": ...} so the educational UI can animate blocks, transactions and peer connections in real time. This channel carries only data that is public on the ledger anyway, so unlike /ws/p2p it is not encrypted at the application layer.

member.name: M:LegalChain.P2p.EventBroadcaster.AttachAsync(System.Net.WebSockets.WebSocket,System.Threading.CancellationToken)
member.summary: Attaches a browser socket and blocks until the client disconnects (the returned task completes when the socket closes).

member.name: T:LegalChain.P2p.JsonDefaults
member.summary: The single JSON dialect of the node — camelCase properties, UPPER_SNAKE enum names — shared by the REST API, the /ws/events feed and the P2P protocol so that every serialized Block/Transaction matches API contract v1 byte-for-byte.

member.name: T:LegalChain.P2p.P2pClientService
member.summary: Outbound side of the P2P mesh: connects this node to a remote peer's /ws/p2p endpoint. The receive loop runs as a thread-pool task (async/await — the .NET analogue of the Java node's Loom virtual threads: cheap, blocking-style I/O), and the moment the socket opens the PQC handshake starts with this node as initiator.

member.name: M:LegalChain.P2p.P2pClientService.ConnectAsync(System.String)
member.summary: Connects to a peer, e.g. ws://localhost:8101/ws/p2p. The handshake continues asynchronously after this method returns.

member.name: T:LegalChain.P2p.P2pService
member.summary: The P2P ledger-synchronization protocol between two remote nodes, secured end-to-end with post-quantum cryptography.

list.item: HELLO (initiator → responder): the initiator's node id, its ML-DSA public key, a fresh ephemeral ML-KEM public key, and an ML-DSA signature over kemPublicKey|nodeId.
ENCAPS (responder → initiator): the responder encapsulates a random 32-byte secret against the initiator's KEM key and returns the ciphertext, signed with its own ML-DSA key over ciphertext|nodeId.
Both sides now hold the same one-time AES-256-GCM session key; every subsequent frame is a SECURE message (authenticated encryption).

remarks.para: Why a man-in-the-middle fails. The classic MITM move is substituting key material in flight. Here both handshake messages are signed with ML-DSA, and each side checks that the claimed node id equals the SHA3-256 fingerprint of the signing key. An attacker who swaps the KEM key or the ciphertext must forge an ML-DSA signature — believed infeasible even for a quantum adversary (lattice hardness, NIST FIPS 204). Tampering with SECURE frames fails the GCM authentication tag instead.

Trust without revealing identity (ZKP-style pseudonymity). Note what the handshake does not contain: names, certificates, IP-bound identities. Each party proves exactly one statement — "I control the private key behind this fingerprint" — by producing a valid signature, and nothing else. This is the zero-knowledge-flavoured privacy model of self-sovereign identity: trust is established between pseudonyms, and even the adopted ledger data is never trusted on channel security alone — re-audits every block, every signature and every consensus proof locally before accepting a peer's chain.

member.name: F:LegalChain.P2p.P2pService.MaxMessageBytes
member.summary: Chain frames carry ~7 KB per signed transaction (ML-DSA sizes) — allow large messages.

member.name: F:LegalChain.P2p.P2pService._peers
member.summary: Live peer links keyed by session id.

member.name: M:LegalChain.P2p.P2pService.ReceiveLoopAsync(LegalChain.P2p.PeerSession,System.Threading.CancellationToken)
member.summary: Reads text frames from the socket until it closes, feeding each into the protocol state machine. Runs on the async thread pool — the .NET analogue of the Java node's Loom virtual threads: blocking-style I/O code, negligible thread cost.

member.name: M:LegalChain.P2p.P2pService.RequireAuthentic(System.String,System.String,System.String,System.String)
member.summary: Verifies the handshake signature AND that the claimed node id is the fingerprint of the signing key — the identity binding that defeats key-substitution MITM.

member.name: M:LegalChain.P2p.P2pService.SyncWithPeersAsync
member.summary: Asks every established peer for its chain; longest valid chain wins locally.

member.name: T:LegalChain.P2p.PeerSession
member.summary: Mutable handshake state for one peer link. A link becomes only after the ML-DSA-authenticated ML-KEM handshake has produced a one-time AES-256 session key; until then no ledger data flows.

member.name: P:LegalChain.P2p.PeerSession.EphemeralKemPrivate
member.summary: Initiator's ephemeral ML-KEM private key, discarded once the session key is derived.

member.name: P:LegalChain.P2p.PeerSession.SendLock
member.summary: Serializes concurrent sends: a WebSocket allows only one outstanding send.

member.name: T:Program
member.summary: Exposed for WebApplicationFactory<Program> in the API contract test.
